content.problemStatement:
Zlatan and Ramos are playing a game on a **binary** string SSS of length NNN.

Zlatan and Ramos make alternating moves with Zlatan going first.

In one move, a player will:


Select an index iii (1≤i<N)(1 \leq i < N)(1≤i<N) such that Si≠Si+1S_i \neq S_{i+1}Si​=Si+1​ and delete both SiS_iSi​ and Si+1S_{i+1}Si+1​ from the string SSS. Note that NNN gets reduced by 222 when both characters are deleted. If a player cannot select any such index iii, he loses the game.
Determine the winner of the game if both players play optimally.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the binary string.
The next line contains a binary string SSS of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, if Zlatan will win the game, output `Zlatan`. Otherwise, output `Ramos`.


You can output each letter of the string in uppercase or lowercase. For example, `Ramos`, `ramos`, `RAMOS`, and `rAmOS` are all considered same.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 300000
S consists only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the binary string. The second line contains a binary string S of length N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing "Zlatan" if Zlatan (the first player) wins with optimal play, otherwise output "Ramos".

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we count zeros and ones. The minimum of the two counts gives the number of possible moves. Its parity decides the winner as described in the approach.

solution.approach: Each move removes exactly one '0' and one '1' because the deleted adjacent characters must be different. Therefore the total number of possible moves equals min(count0, count1). Players alternate moves, so the winner is determined by the parity of this number: if it is odd, the first player (Zlatan) makes the last move and wins; otherwise Ramos wins. The algorithm for each test case is: count zeros and ones, compute m = min(cnt0, cnt1), output "Zlatan" if m is odd else "Ramos".
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string. Overall O(total N) ≤ 3·10^5.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        s = next(it)
        cnt0 = s.count('0')
        cnt1 = N - cnt0
        moves = cnt0 if cnt0 < cnt1 else cnt1
        out.append('Zlatan' if moves % 2 else 'Ramos')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
